coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal vs microwave nanoarchitechtonics of carbon dots significantly affects the structure, physicochemical properties, and anti-cancer activity against a specific neuroblastoma cell line

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) from glucose were synthesized using two of the most common bottom-up methods, namely, microwave assisted (MW) and hydrothermal carbonization (HT). Synthetic parameters such as reaction time, temperature, and precursor concentration were changed to study the effects of each parameter on CD size, structure, surface functionalities, charge, photoluminescence behavior, quantum yield, cytotoxicity, blood–brain barrier (BBB) crossing ability and bioimaging. A detailed analysis is performed to compare the structure and properties of the CDs synthesized in ten different conditions. We show that the synthesis route drastically changes the structure, properties, and related functions of glucose-derived CDs yielding two different subtypes of CDs. Surprisingly, CDs that was synthesized via HT method showed specific anticancer activity against a neuroblastoma cell line while being non-toxic towards healthy cell lines, indicating significant potential for therapeutic applications. CDs synthesized via MW crosses the BBB in zebrafish and rat models, and accumulates in neurons. CDs synthesized via MW method showed high biocompatibility and a great potential to be used for bioimaging applications in vitro and in vivo targeting neurons. Finally, a formation mechanism of CDs is proposed for both HT and MW synthesis routes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV/vis spectra of all HT samples (A), and all MW samples (B), PL emission maxima when excited at 375 nm of all HT samples at the same concentration (C), and PL Intensity of the emission maxima (excited at 375 nm) vs HT sample (at the same concentration, 0.02 mg/ml) (D), PL emission maxima when excited at 375 nm of all MW samples at the same concentration (E), and PL Intensity of the emission maxima (excited at 375 nm) vs MW sample (at the same concentration, 0.01 mg/ml) (F).

PL emission of HT-200–5 at different concentrations between 0.01 and 0.1 mg/ml (excited at 375 nm) (A), PL emission maxima vs concentration plot of HT-200-5 (B), PL emission of MW-10–7 at different concentrations between 0.01 and 2 mg/ml (excited at 375 nm) (C), and PL maxima vs concentration plot of MW-10-7 (D).

TEM images and size distribution histograms of all HT samples: HT-120–5 (A), HT-160–5 (B), HT-200–5 (C), HT-200–10 (D), HT-200–20 (E), and all MW samples: MW-10–7 (F), MW-10–14 (G), MW-20–7 (H), MW-40–7 (I).

Size comparison plots for HT and MW samples with varying parameters. HT samples change in size as a function of reaction time (top left), HT samples change in size as a function of reaction temperature, (bottom left), MW samples change in size as a function of reaction time (top right), MW samples change in size as a function of change in the concentration of the precursor (bottom right).
FTIR spectra of all MW samples (A), FTIR spectra of all HT samples (B), XPS core levels of O 1 s and C 1 s orbitals of MW-10–7 and HT-200–5 CDs (C).
XRD patterns of MW-10–7 (red) and HT-200–5 (blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Thermograms for d-glucose, MW-10–7, and HT-200–5 (A) and their corresponding DTGs (B).
Raman spectra of HT-200–5 (A) and MW-10–7 (B).
Proposed mechanism of formation of CDs synthesized using MW and HT treatments.

Cell viabilities of two healthy (HEK 293 and MSC), two neuroblastoma (SMS-KCNR and SK-N-AS) and one glioblastoma (SJ-GBM2) cell lines, (n = 12 for each cell line except SMS-KCNR, n = 16 for SMS-KCNR), t-test between control (vehicle-treated, 0 μM CDs) and carbon dot-treated (1-, 10-, and 100-μM CDs) groups were conducted (**p < 0.001, *p < 0.05).

With and without DAPI nucleus staining, fluorescence microscopy images of SJ-GBM2 (glioblastoma) cells treated with 10 mM concentration of MW-CDs for 1 h. Scale bars are 15 µm. Excitation wavelength: Blue, 358 nm, Green, 488 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vivo bioimaging – zebrafish: MW-10–7 injected into the heart and central canal is observed under green and white channels (top). Saline is injected into the heart of the control group and central canal is observed under green and white channels (bottom). Scale bars are 26 μm (A), In vivo bioimaging – rat: MW-10–14 (top-right), MW-40–7 (bottom-right) CDs were injected from the tail vein and observed in ventral horn of the cervical spinal cord of rats. Saline was used in the control groups (left). Scalebar is 50 μm (B). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) from glucose were synthesized using two of the most common bottom-up methods, namely, microwave assisted (MW) and hydrothermal carbonization (HT). Synthetic parameters such as reaction time, temperature, and precursor concentration were changed to study the effects of each parameter on CD size, structure, surface functionalities, charge, photoluminescence behavior, quantum yield, cytotoxicity, blood–brain barrier (BBB) crossing ability and bioimaging. A detailed analysis is performed to compare the structure and properties of the CDs synthesized in ten different conditions. We show that the synthesis route drastically changes the structure, properties, and related functions of glucose-derived CDs yielding two different subtypes of CDs. Surprisingly, CDs that was synthesized via HT method showed specific anticancer activity against a neuroblastoma cell line while being non-toxic towards healthy cell lines, indicating significant potential for therapeutic applications. CDs synthesized via MW crosses the BBB in zebrafish and rat models, and accumulates in neurons. CDs synthesized via MW method showed high biocompatibility and a great potential to be used for bioimaging applications in vitro and in vivo targeting neurons. Finally, a formation mechanism of CDs is proposed for both HT and MW synthesis routes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) are the newest class of carbon-based nanoparticles. Since their discovery in early 2000s [1], CDs have attracted tremendous attention because of their unique properties such as small size (<10 nm), high biocompatibility, versatile synthesis, various surface functional groups, bright photoluminescence (PL), and stability [2–6]. Over the past decade, CDs have been studied extensively resulting in a wealth of knowledge about CDs properties and wide variety of applications [7]. There are several studies on how CD synthesis using different precursors as well as various top-down and bottom-up approaches affect the PL behavior, formation mechanism, and biological functions such as therapeutics, bioimaging, and sensing [5,6,8–10]. However, how different synthetic methods and parameters using the sugars as precursors affect the CD structure, physicochemical properties, and relevant biological activity is not yet fully understood. A better understanding of their structure–property relationship will help the field move forward with several applications, especially in biological studies.

There are many options for synthetic methods and reaction conditions for CD preparation. Therefore, the resulting CDs can have varying core structures, which can be classified into four subgroups: graphene quantum dots (GQTs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [11,12]. GQDs are defined as a few layers of graphene sheets with well-defined lattices connected to the functional groups on the edges [13,14,15]. CQDs are spherical nanoparticles with crystal lattices in the core and several functional groups on the surface [16]. CNDs are defined as small, carbonized nanoparticles without distinct crystal lattices in the core and some functional groups on the surface [17]. CPDs have a core with aggregated or cross-linked functional groups or polymer chains on the surface [18]. CNDs and CQDs are considered as completely carbonized, whereas CPDs are considered partially carbonized.

The aim of this study is to determine how synthetic methods change the structure of CDs and which synthesis-dependent physicochemical properties affect their biological function (i.e., anti-tumor activity, blood–brain crossing, and specific cell type targeting). Here, we designed a systematic study in which CDs are prepared using glucose as the sole precursor and two of the most common synthetic methods, namely, hydrothermal carbonization (HT) and microwave-assisted (MW) bottom-up synthesis. We investigated the differences in core structure, surface functional groups, size, charge, and photoluminescent properties, as well as biological activity. We assessed cytotoxicity on healthy and cancer cells, determined the blood–brain barrier (BBB) crossing ability, and utilized the CDs for bioimaging in vitro and in vivo. The differences in preparation methods using a single precursor resulted in substantial differences in structure, properties, and function in biological models. CDs synthesized via HT method showed intrinsic anticancer activity specifically towards one type of neuroblastoma cell line. On the other hand, CDs synthesized via MW route showed high biocompatibility and great potential for neuronal targeting bioimaging applications. Extensive characterization suggested HT and MW synthesis methods yielded in two different subtypes of CDs: carbon nanodots (CNDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), respectively. We also proposed different formation mechanisms of CDs for the two different synthesis methods.

In this study, we used glucose as the sole precursor. We have previously shown that its uptake mechanism requires glucose transporters (GLUTs) [19]. GLUTs are highly relevant for therapeutics development in pathologies such as cancer and stroke. Considering the abundance of glucose receptors on the BBB and cell membranes, this work is of great significance in modern nanomedicine.

d-glucose (99 %) was purchased from VWR International, LLC (Radnor, PA). DI-water was obtained from Direct-Q 3 water purification system (EMD Millipore Corp.; Chicago, IL) with a resistivity of 18 MΩ·cm at 20.0 ± 0.5 °C and surface tension of 71.2 mN·m−1 at the same temperature. NaOH pellets (98.5 %) (nitrogen flushed, hygroscopic) were obtained from Acros Organics, Fisher Scientific (Pittsburgh, PA). Pre-treated Spectra/por7 dialysis membrane tubing, 1 kDa MWCO, was purchased from Spectrum Laboratories Supply, Inc (Rancho Dominguez, CA). Syringe filters (0.2 μm) were acquired from VWR (Radnor, PA). Muffle furnace (BF51800 Series) from Thermo Scientific (Rockford, IL) was utilized to run the reactions using a Teflon coated stainless steel hydrothermal synthesis reactor from Baoshishan (DW-5 MB2-BG61). FreeZone 4.5 L cascade benchtop freeze dry system from the Labconco Co. (Kansas City, MO) was used to lyophilize the samples. A Branson 1510 ultrasonicator (Gaithersburg, MD) was employed to sonicate aqueous CD samples prior to characterization. A ceramic crucible with lid (Millipore-Sigma, St. Louis, MO) was used for microwave reactions along with a domestic microwave oven. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

CDs were prepared following our previously reported protocol with modifications in the reaction temperature and time [3]. Briefly, 2 g of d-glucose was dissolved in 20 ml DI-H2O and stirred for 15 min until a homogenous solution formed. The solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated in a muffle furnace at different temperatures and reaction times (Figure S1). Each reaction was repeated at least three times. Sample IDs and reaction conditions are given in Table S1. After the completing of the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C, filtered through a 0.2-µm syringe filter, adjusted to neutral pH, and dialyzed using a 1 kDa MWCO dialysis membrane against DI-H2O for three days changing the dialysis water every 24 h. Finally, the products were freeze dried to yield the solid product. Powdered product was weighed to find the product yield. Product yield was reported as an average of at least three batches with standard error of the mean.

Sample IDs for the MW preparation along with precursor concentration and duration of the reaction are given in Table S2. The samples were prepared by dissolving d-glucose in DI-H2O of desired concentration and stirring for 15 min. Then, the solution was transferred to a crucible in a domestic microwave oven at 700 W for the desired amount of time (Figure S1). The purification steps were the same as those used in the HT carbonization approach. Each reaction was repeated at least three times. Powdered product was weighed to find the product yield. Product yield was reported as an average of at least three batches with standard error of the mean.

A Cary 100 Ultraviolet/visible (UV/vis) spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Inc.; Santa Clara, CA, USA) was used to acquire UV/vis absorption spectra of CDs dispersed in DI-H2O using a semi micro quartz cuvette with 1 cm pathlength and two dark walls (Starna Cells, Inc). A Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 (Horiba, ltd.; Piscataway, NJ, USA) was used for photoluminescent (PL) emission spectra with a slit width of 5 nm for both excitation and emission. A quartz cuvette with all clear walls and 1-cm pathlength was used for the PL emission measurements. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy equipped with attenuated total reflection accessories (FTIR-ATR, Perkin Elmer, Inc.; Waltham, MA, USA) was used for investigation of lyophilized CDs with air as background.

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using a Perkin Elmer PHI 560 system with a double-pass cylindrical mirror analyzer using a Mg Kα anode with a hν = 1253.6 eV photon energy operated at 250 W and 13 kV. Solid CDs were mounted on a custom-built sample holder and via turbo-pumped antechamber was employed to insert solid CDs into the XPS system. The system pressure was not more than 1 × 10–8 Torr during scans. C 1 s core level at 284.9 eV emanating from the CC alkenyl groups of the carbon dots matched with those previously measured in our laboratory [20]. This binding energy (BE) peak center was used as a reference. Core level intensities of the O 1 s and C 1 s orbitals were normalized to their known atomic sensitivity factors [21]. XPS spectra were curve fitted using 70 %-to-30 % Gaussian-Lorentzian lineshapes with Shirley background subtractions [22]. CasaXPS ver. 2.2.107 (Devonshire, UK) software was used to deconvolute the BE peak envelopes.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Agilent 5420) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL 1200x) were used for morphological studies. Approximately 20 μL ofaqueousCDssolution was placed on a clean mica slide and air dried for AFM sample preparation followed by putting the mica slideunder the silicon AFM tip (length: 225 µm; thickness: 5 µm; force constant: 15 N/m) and imagingwith tapping mode. A drop of the aqueousCDs solution was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid and air dried before TEM imaging. ImageJ processing software was used to measure the diameter of CDs to plot a size distribution histogram. Measurements were repeated three times.

Zeta potentials were measured using a DLS nano series Malvern Zetasizer (Westborough, MA). Raman spectra were recorded using a Renishaw Inviah Spectrometer equipped with a Blue (457 nm), Green (514.5 nm), and Red (785 nm) laser source and a 50x objective. Power was applied using the blue laser line from 100 μW to 1 mW, the green laser from 10 to 100 μW, and the red laser line from 50 to 500 μW.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was collected using a Rigaku TTRIII polycrystal X-ray diffraction equipped at 18 kW with Cu Kα radiation. Bragg’s law was used to calculate the d-spacing: nλ = 2d⋅sin(θ), where n is order of reflection, λ is the wavelength of the X-ray beam (0.154 nm), d is the distance between the adjacent sheets or layers, and θ is the diffraction angle. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of samples was conducted using a Netzsch TG 209 F3 Tarsus thermo-microbalance while heating from 40 to 1000 °C (10 °C/min increments) under nitrogen flow.

The human embryonic kidney cell line HEK-293 was obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas,VA, U.S.A.). Human mesenchymal stem cells (MSC) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). The pediatric glioblastoma cell line SJGBM2 and SMS-KCNR were obtained from the Children’s Oncology Group (COG, Lubbock, TX). Neuroblastoma cell lines SK-N-AS was obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) medium, Minimum Essential Media (MEM), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Fisher Scientific (USA). Penicillin and streptomycin (P/S) were purchased from Gemini Biosciences (West Sacramento, CA). The CellTiter 96 Aqueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay (MTS) was acquired from Promega (Madison, WI). All cells were kept in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C, which were routinely tested for mycoplasma using LookOut mycoplasma PCR detection kit (Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) according to manufacturer instructions.

HEK-293 (human embryonic kidney), SJGBM2 (pediatric glioblastoma), SK-N-AS (neuroblastoma), and SMS-KCNR (neuroblastoma) cells were seeded into 96-well plates in 100 μL of cell culture media (Roswell Park Memorial Institute, RPMI, Gibco) containing 10 % FBS and 1 % P/S. The MEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % P/S was used for MSC (mesenchymal stem cells). The density of the cells per well was 0.5x105 for HEK-293 and MSC, 1x105 for SJGBM2 and SMS-KCNR, and 1.5x105 for SK-N-AS. Cells were incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h followed by treatment with 100 μL CDs for a final concentration of 1, 10 and 100 μM. Cell viability was determined with MTS assay after 72 h of incubation. A BioTek Synergy HT plate reader was used to read absorbance at 490 and 690 nm. Viability was calculated as the percent of non-treated cells. Data is reported as the average of 3 separate experiments (n = 12) with the standard error of the mean. Consistency and reproducibility of the data was confirmed using different batches of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: SJ-GBM2 cells were plated in 24-well plates (NUNC, Denmark) for imaging at a density of 1 × 105 in 500 µL in RPMI. Prior to CD treatment, these cells were incubated for 24 h for sufficient cell growth. Then, the medium was aspirated out, and cells were treated with 500 µL RPMI containing 10 mM of CDs. After 1 h incubation with CDs, cells were washed with PBS and fixed with fresh 4 % paraformaldehyde for another 30 min. Subsequently, 4 % paraformaldehyde was discarded, and fixed cells were washed with PBS solution two more times. Then, one drop of antifade reagent with DAPI (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) was introduced on the top of the cells. For each well, one cover slip was added carefully on top to avoid bubbles before imaging. A Leica SP5 confocal microscope (Leica Microsystems Inc., Buffalo Grove, IL) was operated for imaging by using fluorescent channels with the excitation wavelengths of Blue, 405 nm, and Green, 488 nm. Emission ranges were Blue, 420–460 nm, and Green, 495–595 nm. Experimental settings were kept constant for each group.

For in vivo bioimaging studies using rats, same protocol was used as in our previous report [19]. Briefly, adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (Envigo; Indianapolis, IN), weighing 400 g, were used to test if CDs crossed the BBB. Experimental protocols were approved by the University of Florida Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee and conducted in accordance with the Animal Welfare Act, the Public Health Service Policy on Humane Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, and the NIH Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (2011). Rats were kept at 12-h light–dark cycle and had access to food and water ad libitum. All injections and terminal procedures were performed under anesthesia. Anesthesia was induced (3 %) and maintained (2–2.5 %) with isoflurane. The depth of anesthesia was assessed by the absence of the toe pinch reflex.After the lateral tail vein was catheterized (24 Gauge; Surflash, Somerset, NJ), CDs in sterile saline (or vehicle) were administered over a duration of 5 min. Then, anesthesia was discontinued. Rats were kept awake and monitored regularly for 4 h where no abnormal signs were observed. Rats were sacrificed by transcardial perfusion using chilled 0.01 M PBS at 4 °C followed by 4 % paraformaldehyde in 0.01 M PBS (4 % PFA; pH 7.4). Brain and spinal cord tissues were harvested, post‐fixed in 4 % PFA (4 °C, pH 7.4) overnight, and cryoprotected in 30 % sucrose solution (4 °C). C4 to C6 spinal cord segments were sectioned transversely to 40-μm thick slices using a freezing microtome (SM2010R, Leica; Buffalo Grove, IL). Three slices per spinal segment were uniformly sampled, washed, and mounted on charged slides with hard-set anti-fade media (Vector Labs; Burlingame, CA). Slides were imaged at 20 × magnification with a fluorescence microscope (BZ-X710; Keyence Co.; Osaka, Japan) using a GFP filter (BZ-X, model no: OP-87763). The same exposure times were used across all groups.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using two commonly used bottom-up methods, namely, microwave assisted (MW) and hydrothermal carbonization (HT) treatment in a Teflon coated autoclave reactor. The reaction conditions were changed to study the effects on the CD’s structure and properties (Table S1 and Table S2). The goal of this study was to establish the limits of the formation of the CDs and optimize the sought properties for various applications such as drug delivery and bioimaging. Table S3 lists all samples’ yield of products, zeta potentials and fluorescence quantum yields (QYs). Each type of CD was prepared in at least three independent batches to ensure the consistency and reproducibility of the reactions.

For HT reactions, the concentration of the precursor was kept the same (10 wt%) for all conditions, whereas the reaction temperature (120, 160, and 200 °C) and duration (5, 10, and 20 h) were changed. For MW synthesis, the power was kept the same (700 W) for all reactions, whereas the concentration of the precursor (10, 20 and 40 wt%) and the duration of the reaction was changed (5, 7, and 14 min).

In addition, two reactions with 10 wt% precursor concentration were run for 3.5- and 21-minutes using the MW method. However, no CDs were formed at 3.5 min, while the reaction for 21 min produced mostly burnt charcoal like product which was not soluble in water and not investigated. The product yield for the 5 min reaction (0.44 mg) was substantially low compared to all the other MW samples (>330 mg), suggesting that the formation of CDs potentially begins around 5 min.

The physical appearance, UV/vis absorption and PL emission spectra of the 5 min sample were also different from the rest. When removed from the microwave, the physical appearance of the MW-10–5 sample was a very pale yellow, whereas all the other samples were dark brown. MW-10–7 and MW-10–14 samples had similar product yields (∼400 mg). On the other hand, yields of MW-20–7 (751 mg) and MW-40–7 (1947 mg) were substantially higher than MW-10–7 (381 mg), indicating that increasing the precursor concentration had a larger effect on product yield than extending the reaction time. Zeta potentials of CDs synthesized via MW method were between negative 4 and 22. Absolute values of zeta potentials of MW samples were generally lower than those of the CDs synthesized via HT method. The highest fluorescence QY was obtained from the HT-120–5 (8.2 %) sample with the lowest product yield per reaction. The fluorescence QY of MW samples were relatively similar at around 2–3 %. Generally, MW samples had higher fluorescence QY compared to HT samples. Similar to the MW-10–5 sample, the physical appearance of the HT-120–5 sample was a very pale yellow after the reaction. Combined with a very low product yield, it is likely the lower limit for the formation of CDs. Below 120 °C for the same duration of the reaction, CDs were unlikely to form. A reaction for 3.5 h had been run and no CDs were formed. The product yield of the HT samples was not significantly different from each other except HT-120–5 sample which had the lowest product yield (<1 mg). There was a large difference in the yield between HT and MW samples where the product yield of MW samples (>300 mg) was significantly larger than those of the HT samples (<15 mg).

Fig. 1 A shows Ultraviolet/visible (UV/vis) spectra for all samples prepared via HT reactions. Fig. 1B shows UV/vis spectra of all samples prepared via MW-assisted approach. The band around 260–325 nm can be attributed to the π- π* transition (CC), whereas the band around 325–400 nm can be attributed to the n- π* transition (CO) [3]. For HT reactions, HT-120–5 and HT-200–20 showed slightly different spectra as compared to the HT-160–5, HT-200–5 and HT-200–10 samples. This finding suggests that all CDs have similar functional groups, but not the same structure. Similarly, MW samples have two shoulder-like bands around the same region centered around 275 and 350 nm. The MW-10–5 sample showed a slightly different spectrum compared to the rest of the samples, suggesting a structural difference. Considering that MW-10–5 and HT-120–5 underwent the mildest reaction conditions, these samples may not have had the same amount of carbonization and/or surface functional groups as compared to the rest of the samples. On the other hand, HT-200–20 sample had the harshest reaction conditions for the HT method, which may have resulted in higher degrees of carbonization.

Figure S2 shows the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of all samples prepared via HT method. All samples have excitation-dependent emission. HT-120–5 sample has an emission maximum at 380 nm when excited at 300 nm. This is different than the other glucose-derived CDs prepared with hydrothermal method. HT-160–5 sample has the emission maximum at 465 nm when excited at 375 nm. HT-200–5, HT-200–10, and HT-200–20 samples all have emission maxima between 445 and 465 nm when excited at 350 or 375 nm. Figure S3 shows the PL emission spectra of all MW samples at two different concentrations, namely 1 mg/ml (left column) and 0.1 mg/ml (right column). All samples except MW-10–5 have a red shift in emission maxima when the concentration was higher.

At the 0.1 mg/ml concentration, all MW samples had the maximum emission with an excitation at 375 nm. The maximum emission, when excited at 375 nm, changed from 450 to 470 nm for the MW samples Figure S3 (B, D, F, H, and J). PL emission spectra of all MW samples were almost identical at the lower concentration (0.1 mg/ml aqueous dispersion). On the other hand, the PL emission maxima and the corresponding excitation wavelengths varied for different samples at the higher concentration (1 mg/ml aqueous dispersion). When excited at 400 nm, the emission maximum for MW-10–7 was at 495 nm, for MW-10–14 emission maximum was at 503 nm, and MW-20–7 had the emission maximum at 508 nm. The emission maximum of MW-40–7 was at 520 nm when excited at 425 nm. The red shift in emission maxima in all samples dependent on the concentration can be explained by the concentration-dependent red shift. Another possible explanation is the possibility of crosslink enhanced emission effect in MW samples [11,14].

When the emission maxima were compared for all HT samples excited at 375 nm, the HT-120–5 sample had the highest intensity and hence the highest fluorescence QY. The intensity of the other HT samples didn’t change significantly (Fig. 1C-D). When the emission maxima were compared for all MW samples excited at 375 nm, the MW-20–7 sample had the highest intensity. All MW samples except MW-10–5 had the emission maxima at 475 nm when excited at 375 nm. MW-10–5 sample had the emission max at 450 nm when excited at 375 nm (Fig. 1E-F). Furthermore, the PL emission maximum of HT-200–5 sample showed a concentration dependent change in the PL in the range of concentrations between 0.01 and 0.1 mg/ml (Fig. 2 A). The PL intensity of HT-200–5 sample increased for concentrations between 0.01 and 0.05 mg/ml. Between 0.05 and 0.1 mg /ml, the PL intensity decreased (Fig. 2B), suggesting a concentration dependent quenching of PL emission.

Fig. 2C shows the PL emission of MW-10–7 at various concentrations (0.1–2 mg/ml) when excited at 375 nm. Fig. 2D exhibits the emission maxima vs concentration plot. The MW-10–7 sample showed an increase in the PL emission intensity with increasing concentration in the range of 0.1 to 1 mg/ml with a plateau between 1 and 2 mg/ml.

Significant differences in PL behavior of HT and MW samples suggest that two synthetic routes yield two structurally different subtypes of CDs. HT-CDs may be classified as CNDs while MW-CDs may fall under the CPD subgroup based on the XRD, FTIR and Raman spectroscopic data [11,14]. Moreover, detailed studies using HR-TEM can help a more definitive classification by investigating crystal lattices of the core. The luminescence of HT-CDs samples as CNDs may mainly originate from the surface and subdomain states within the core, not having quantum confinement effect of the particle size [17]. On the other hand, MW-CD samples, similar to CPDs, may have molecular structures incorporated into the polymer/carbon hybrid skeleton, serving as fluorescence centers to contribute to the enhancement of luminescence. The crosslinking structure can restrict the vibration and rotation to reduce the nonradiative relaxation processes, resulting in increased emission probability contributing to PL [11,14]. The CPDs not only possess the prominent optical and unique PL properties, they have characteristics such as high oxygen content, which is supported for the MW-CD samples by XPS data [11,14]. This hypothesis is supported by spectroscopic analysis (vide infra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy and atomic force microscopy studies
ce:section.ce:para: Figure 3A-E and Fig. 3 F-I show representative transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and size distributions of HT and MW samples, respectively. To plot the size distribution histograms, at least 300 particles per sample were counted to have a good representation of the particle population. Table S4 shows the mean diameters of all samples synthesized via HT or MW assisted methods.

The plots in Fig. 4 show changes in the sizes of CDs synthesized as a function of various parameters. The reaction time for HT did not change the size of HT samples at 200 °C for 5 and 10 h. When the reaction was run for 20 h the mean diameter was increased to 14.67 nm making the nanoparticles larger than the traditionally defined CD particle size range (<10 nm). The longer reaction time may have allowed for a higher degree of carbonization and thus the formation of larger particles. Also, the skewed shape of the size distribution (Fig. 3E) suggests the possibility of aggregation of the HT-200–20 CDs. For HT samples, changing the reaction temperature from 120 to 160 and 200 °C, respectively, did not result in a significant difference in the average size of the CDs.

On the other hand, changing reaction time and precursor concentration resulted in significant changes in the mean diameter of MW-CDs. The increase in the reaction time (MW-10–14) resulted in CDs with a mean diameter larger than 10 nm. No particles were detected in the MW-10–5 samples either with TEM or AFM. Therefore, only the MW-10–7 and MW-10–14 samples were compared. The size of the MW-CDs increased when the precursor concentration was increased from 10 to 20 %. However, a further increase in the precursor concentration to 40 % did not result in larger particles. Instead, when the precursor concentration was at 40 %, the average diameter was the smallest when the reaction time was kept constant. Table S4 shows the mean diameter size of all samples.

Figure S4 A-E shows representative atomic force microscopy (AFM) images and extracted profiles of HT samples, and Figure S4 F-I shows representative AFM images and extracted profiles of MW samples, except for the MW-10–5 sample. Figure S4-J is a table which shows the average height of the CDs for each sample. Height profiles from the AFM images of HT samples are in good agreement with TEM images for all samples suggesting HT-CDs have near-spherical shapes. On the other hand, AFM images of MW samples showed more variability and a tendency towards aggregation. In particular, MW-20–7 sample showed larger clumps in agreement with the mean diameter increase based on TEM images. However, the height profile showed CDs with smaller sizes in the z-direction, revealing either a disk-like shape of CDs or aggregation. Other MW samples resulted in similar height profiles as compared to their mean diameter based on TEM.

Fig. 5 A presents the attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of all CDs prepared via MW (except MW-10–5). Fig. 5B is the FTIR spectra of all CDs prepared via HT treatment (except HT-120–5). The yields of MW-10–5 and HT-120–5 reactions were too low, such that there were insufficient lyophilized samples for FTIR analysis. The spectra show a wide prominent peak centered at around 3335–3386 cm−1, suggesting the presence of hydroxyl groups (OH) in all samples. The peaks at 2927–2932 cm−1 can be attributed to saturated νC-H. The peaks at 1703–1705 cm−1 show the presence of carbonyl groups (νC=O) while peaks at 1610–1633 cm−1 suggest the formation of CC bonds. The bands around 1357–1416 cm−1 correspond to bending modes in the CO and CC groups. The sharp peak at 1020 cm−1 is mainly from the hydroxylic CO single bond stretching of the COC group suggesting the presence of anhydroglucose rings. All the MW samples show a prominent COC peak. Interestingly, the intensity of the peak at 1020 cm−1 in the HT samples decreased with increasingly harsher reaction conditions. In other words, when the reaction temperature and the duration of the reaction were increased, the intensity of the peak decreased until no apparent peak was observed for the HT-200–20 sample.

Similar spectra were collected for HT and MW samples suggesting that similar functional groups were present. However, two main differences can be appreciated owing to differences in the intensity of νC=O and νC-O-C. HT spectra showed a more intense νC=O compared to νC-H and a much less intense νC-O-C in comparison to the MW samples suggesting that even if the functional groups of HT and MW samples were similar, the structures were not. This postulate is consistent with the differences in UV/vis absorption and PL emission spectra. Figure S5 shows an overlay of FTIR spectra of HT-200–5 and MW-10–7 samples for easy comparison.
From this point forward, we have selected one HT (HT-200–5) and one MW (MW-7–10) sample for further, more detailed characterization using XPS, XRD and Raman spectroscopy.

Fig. 5C summarizes the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data, showing stack plots for the O 1s and C 1s core levels of the MW-10–7 and HT-200–5 CDs. Atom mole fraction results are summarized in Table S5. C 1s core level Bes for all samples were relatively constant. While the C 1 s BE at 284.9 eV matches alkenyl C (vide supra), BE at 286.9 eV is consistent with an anhydro glucose ring (COC) [24]. A broad shake-up feature of C 1s oxidation state at 290.6 eV was observed in MW-10–7 CDs [25], denoting the presence of a conjugated system. The O 1 s oxidation state at 532.8 eV is indicative of hydroxyl oxygen on the surface of HT-200–5 CDs [20,26]. Similarly, the oxidation state at 534.4 on the surface of MW-10–7 CDs also represents hydroxyl oxygen [27]. MW-10–7 and HT-200–5 CDs differ in the relative percent areas of two distinct chemical oxidation states for oxygen. The O 1s BE peak center at 531.2 eV [28] and 533.2 eV [29], observed in both CD structures, is indicative of carbonyl oxygen (CO) bound to the C dot surface, with relative peak areas of 87.6 and 7.85 % from MW-10–7 and HT-200–5 CDs, respectively. The percentages of hydroxyl oxygen (OH) present in MW-10–7 and HT-200–7 CDs were 12.4 and 92.1 %, respectively. The relative C 1s peak envelope areas of this oxidation state were 65.4 and 26.1 % for the MW-10–7 CDs and HT-200–5 CDs, respectively. Noteworthy is the fact that COC carbon envelope (C 1s BE = 286.9 eV) for MW-10–7 CDs was at least 2.5 times greater than those of HT-200–5 CDs, consistent with the FTIR spectra.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of MW-10–7 (red) and HT-200–5 (blue) are shown in Fig. 6 . Single broad peaks appear at around 2θ value of 20–22.5°, suggesting that both samples have amorphous cores. The d-spacing of HT-200–5 and MW-10–7 samples were calculated as 0.39 and 0.44 nm, respectively. This result is not consistent with d-spacing of graphite ∼ 0.33–0.36 nm [30], which is often attributed to the effects of internal stress and heteroatomic doping: such large deviations do not normally occur in either case. XRD patterns of HT-200–5 and MW-10–7 samples are consistent with previous reports of saccharide-derived CDs’ XRD patterns which also suggest amorphous structures with significant level of topological disorder [31]. For HT-200–5 sample, the 002 peak at about 2θ = 22.5° suggests turbostratic carbons with a set of sp2 carbons-graphitic carbons with stacking faults [30,31].

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) were conducted on d-glucose and CD samples, namely MW-10–7 and HT-200–5, to discern their respective surface compositions and compare their thermal stabilities. Nitrogen gas was used to avoid oxidation of the glucose and the CDs during heating. Fig. 7 A presents the d-glucose sample didn’t decompose at low temperatures in comparison to the CD samples, which demonstrates little water molecules attached to the precursor. Fig. 7B presents three decomposition stages of d-glucose. The first stage ranged between 40 and 253 °C with a mass loss of 34 % due to the loss of water molecules. Considering the mass loss didn’t occur below 100 °C, the water molecules lost in the first stage were likely to be formed due to dehydration of d-glucose. The second stage was between 253 and 398 °C with a drastic mass loss of 36 % due to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH) and carbonyl (CO) groups and the release of CO2 [32,33]. The third stage between 398 and 642 °C indicates a complete decomposition of the rest sp2 carbons formed with dehydration reactions during the first stage and sp3 carbons derived from d-glucose [34]. In addition, with the TGA and DTG of d-glucose as references, Fig. 7B shows three stages of MW-10–7 CDs in a complete decomposition, namely 40–152, 152–398, and 398–503 °C. According to Fig. 7A, the initial slight mass loss of 9 % before 152 °C was due to the evaporation of water molecules adsorbed by MW-10–7 CDs. In comparison, when the temperature was increased from 152 to 398 °C, the mass loss of MW-10–7 CDs achieved up to 68 %, which resulted from the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional moieties such as carbonyl groups (CO), CO and COC that were confirmed by FTIR and XPS analyses while emitting CO2. And the third stage (398–503 °C) exhibits an appreciable mass loss of 23 %, which could correspond to the decomposition of sp2 and sp3 carbons previously shown by FTIR, XPS, and XRD on the surfaces or in the cores of MW-10–7 CDs as well as aromatic fluorophores incorporated in the matrix of MW-CDs. Furthermore, the DTG measurement of HT-200–5 CDs in Fig. 7B exhibits four decomposition stages. In Fig. 7A, the first stage (40–137 °C) shows a slight mass loss (10 %) caused by the evaporation of water molecules adsorbed on HT-200–5 [35,36]. The second stage (137–373 °C) with a mass loss of 43 % was due to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups such as CO, CO and COC [37,38]. A lower mass loss of 43 % in HT-200–5 CDs at this stage than MW-10–7 CDs reveals a lower content of CO and COC, which is consistent with a lower FTIR peak intensity ratio of νC-O-C to νC-H of HT-200–5 CDs (Fig. 5). With one of our previous studies [34] as a reference, the third stage between 373 and 438 °C with a mass loss of 24 % was also due to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups and their thermostabilities might be significantly enhanced by the connected structures. The last stage between 438 and 523 °C shows a mass loss of 17 % due to the decomposition of sp2 and sp3 carbons demonstrated by FTIR, XPS, and XRD analyses. After 523 °C, the residue with a constant mass percentage of 6 % was hard to decompose. In comparison, considering the carbonization process to fabricate both CDs, their structures and thermal stabilities were both different from that of pristine d-glucose. Also, the extent of carbonization seems to be higher in HT-200–5 than in MW-10–7 CDs, so there was a stable undecomposed remnant in HT-200–5 upon heating up to 1000 °C, which was confirmed by XPS measurements (Tables S5). The thermal stability of HT CDs was higher than that of MW CDs, and in terms of structure, HT treatment led to a more complicated structure than MW treatment reflecting the increasing decomposition stages.

As explained before, FTIR/ATR spectra of HT-200–5 and MW-10–7 showed similar functional groups; however, the difference in the intensities suggested structural differences. This was evident by the comparison of Raman spectra reported in Fig. 8 . The spectrum of HT-200–5 (Fig. 8A) clearly displayed D and G peaks [39] typical of condensed aromatic carbon structure, while the MW-10–7 spectrum displayed only photoluminescence (Fig. 8B).

According to Tagliaferro et al. [40], the spectrum of HT-200–5 was fitted using GauLor shaped components obtaining an ID/IG ratio of 1.18, and a not well-resolved signal in the region between 2500 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1, suggesting that the aromatic carbon structures formed a long-range disordered material in HT-200–5 sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of formation of CDs via HT and MW methods
ce:section.ce:para: Considering XPS and spectroscopical analysis, we hypothesize a complex chemical pathway for the formation of HT and MW CDs as the one reported in Fig. 9 . By using MW irradiation, glucose molecule underwent a radical cleavage of acetal CO bond forming 1, an intermediate that could be detected by HPLC analysis as reported by Bartoli et al. [41]. This species underwent a further multistep dehydration process forming the enone named 4.

This compound could produce single ring aromatics [41] through several dehydration steps or condense through aldol reaction forming species such as 7 and 8. These could be converted into polyhydroxylic aromatic species such as 11. All aromatics and highly unsaturated species formed through this pathway could form a complex structure where they are connected to several intermediates and glucose residues through COC bonds. This is due to the relatively low energy required to form a phenoxyl radical (around 79 ∼ 93 Kcal/mol) and its high reactivity [42].

Nonetheless, glucose could also undergo a simple dehydration process producing 12–15. This route led to the formation of 16 and 17 which can account for the IR bands of νC=O. Similar to the other route, these species could condense through aldol process forming species such as 19 that easily led to the formation of less functionalized aromatic cores. On the external edges of this structures, the enlargement continued through both dehydration and aldol reactions.

Considering all these data, it is reasonable to assume that the pathway based on the condensation of 12–15 was the most representative of the production of HT-CDs. The aromatic cores formed during the production of 19 analogs were accounted for the Raman spectra, while the higher relative intensity of νC=O supported the massive presence of ester intermediates.

During the production by MW, it is reasonable to assume that both processes took place with the radical rearrangement, however, being the more relevant. This assertion is supported by the high relative intensity of the νC-O-C peak as a result of the radical rearrangement of aromatic structures through the formation of phenoxy-type bonds. Furthermore, the massive presence of phenolic functions justified the intense fluorescence detected during the Raman analysis.

Aromatic rich domains were present in HT as a consequence of the condensation of 19, and its analogues explained the improved thermal stability compared with phenoxy-based structures forming MW. These different chemical pathways originating from HT and MW can be ascribed to the short reaction time and the high energy intensity of MW synthesis that promoted radical cleavage over the advanced aldol condensation.

Cell viability experiments were completed using two healthy (HEK 293 and MSC), two neuroblastoma (SMS-KCNR and SK-N-AS) and one glioblastoma (SJGBM2) cell lines (n = 12 for each cell line except SMS-KCNR, n=16 for SMS-KCNR) (Fig. 10 ). Two of the HT (HT-160–5 and HT-200–5) and two of the MW (MW-10–7 and MW-40–7) samples were selected to carry out the cytotoxicity experiments. Cells were treated with various concentrations (1, 10, and 100 μM) of MW and HT samples in a 96-well plate and incubated for three days. The MTS assay was used for the assessment of CD toxicity in which the cell viability is calculated compared to the non-treated cells.

CDs synthesized via MW-assisted approach did not show any significant toxicity towards any of the cell lines in any concentration, suggesting that MW-CDs have excellent biocompatibility. For all cell lines except SMS-KCNR, the viability of the cells which were treated with HT samples showed a decrease to 85–50 % only for the highest concentration (100 μM). The lower concentration of HT samples did not show significant toxicity. Strikingly, HT-200–5 and HT-160–5 samples showed significant toxicity specifically towards the neuroblastoma cell line SMS-KCNR while no toxicity towards the healthy cell lines was measured. The cell viability of SMS-KCNR neuroblastoma cells decreased significantly even with the lowest concentration (1 μM) of the HT-CDs. In comparison, the highest toxicity towards SMS-KCNR neuroblastoma cells from the HT-160–5 samples occurred with a concentration of 10 μM. We have repeated these experiments with different batches of HT-CDs and different batches of SMS-KCNR cell lines to ensure reproducibility. On the other hand, no significant toxicity from either HT or MW samples is observed for the second neuroblastoma cell line, SK-N-AS.

We speculate that difference in cell viability in two neuroblastoma cell lines is due to the different gene mutations of the cell lines. In general, pediatric cancers, including neuroblastoma, demonstrate significantly fewer genetic changes (mutational burden) compared to adult cancers [43]. Of these, MYCN amplification is the most significant single gene prognostic factor for neuroblastoma and is associated with treatment resistance and poor survival [44]. Furthermore, MYCN is an oncogenic driver and targeting MYCN induces cell death both in vitro and tumor suppression in vivo [45–47]. SMS-KCNR demonstrates MYCN amplification whereas SK-N-AS (and the other cell lines tested) do not. It is tempting to speculate that HT-200 5 h and HT-160 5 h may target MYCN which would explain the robust cell death observed only in this cell line. Long considered an “undruggable” target, successful inhibition of MYCN signaling would offer new hope to children diagnosed with MYCN amplified neuroblastoma. This interesting anticancer property of HT-CDs is planned to be investigated in more detail in future studies. Nonetheless, only HT samples showed an anticancer effect for the SMS-KCNR cells while MW samples showed no toxicity suggesting structural differences between HT and MW samples consistent will all other characterization data. Less toxicity of MW-CDs, as compared to HT-CDs, may stem from the structural differences of the core and surface [48]. Furthermore, this difference in the structure of HT and MW samples resulted in a significant difference in their functions as potential therapeutics toward neuroblastoma and potentially other cancer types harboring MYCN amplification.

MW-CDs were employed as bioimaging tools using glioblastoma cell line, SJGBM2. The HT-200–5 sample was prepared using the same conditions as the GluCDs we reported previously [19]. We successfully showed that GluCDs prepared with the HT route cross the BBB in vivo and accumulate in DRG neurons in vitro. However, HT-GluCDs have very low QY, thus the brightness was not enough for fluorescence bioimaging. Therefore, we conjugated the GluCDs to fluorescein for bioimaging, GluCD-F [19]. In contrast, glucose-based CDs prepared via MW-assisted method showed higher QY values (Table S3). Furthermore, MW samples also showed an increase in PL emission with increasing concentration. This concentration-dependent PL emission enhancement combined with the non-toxicity based on the cytotoxicity studies suggest that MW-CDs are excellent for bioimaging applications. We used SJGBM2 glioblastoma cells as the model system. The cells were incubated with MW-40–7 CDs for 1 h and then imaged with or without DAPI staining (Fig. 11 ). Green channel showed fluorescence from MW-CDs treated cells while no fluorescence was detected from the control groups. The morphology of the cells was normal, consistent with the non-toxic nature of the MW samples. To study the overall cell body and nucleus distribution of MW-CDs within the SJGBM2 cells, we performed bioimaging studies with and without the presence of DAPI nucleus staining dye. We observed that MW-CDs can successfully distribute through overall cell structure, including the nucleus. The ability to colocalize with the nucleus can provide tremendous advantages for any CD designed to use for drug delivery. While cell distribution traits of MW-CDs are very promising for future drug delivery projects, further studies should be performed to fully understand their cellular uptake and distribution behaviors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo Bioimaging: Zebrafish and rat studies
ce:section.ce:para: We used wild-type zebrafish Danio rerio as an in vivo model for crossing BBB to test whether the MW-10–7 sample can cross the BBB and can be detected via confocal microscopy without the need of conjugation to a dye such as fluorescein. HT-200–5 sample was prepared using the same conditions as the GluCDs we reported previously, and we showed that the fluorescein conjugate of CDs, GluCD-F, can cross the BBB. No fluorescence from HT-GluCDs was detected without the fluorescein conjugation [19].

Here, we tested if MW-10–7 passes the BBB of zebrafish and could be detected without conjugation of the fluorescein. A 15 mg/mL aqueous dispersion of MW-10–7 was injected into the heart of the zebrafish (n = 12) and the central canal was observed. Green fluorescence was observed in the central canal of the spinal cord of zebrafish under a confocal microscope with the excitation at 405 nm. The observation area is shown in the white square in Fig. 12 -A. On the contrary, the control group did not show detectable fluorescence. Therefore, zebrafish model showed that CDs prepared using glucose via a MW route could also cross the BBB like the GluCDs prepared using HT method. Furthermore, MW-CDs were detected without the need of conjugation to a dye. The non-toxic property, ability to cross the BBB and brighter luminescence of glucose-derived CDs using MW method shows promise for use as a bioimaging tool of CNS related studies in vivo.

We used rats as the second in vivo model to study the BBB crossing property of MW-CDs. MW-10–14 and MW-40–7 samples were injected to the tail vein of rats for bioimaging to test 1) whether they can cross BBB and be detected without any further conjugation to a dye such as fluorescein, 2) observe the cell type accumulation, and 3) if the size of the CDs has any effect on the cellular uptake.

The ventral horn of the cervical spinal cord of rats was used for imaging. Fig. 12-B shows the fluorescence microscopic images of rats treated with MW-10–14 (top) and MW-40–7 (bottom). Both CDs were observed in the ventral horn of the cervical spinal cord of the rats while the control groups (saline injection) did not show any fluorescence. The morphology of the cells was consistent with neurons suggesting that MW-CDs, like GluCD-F in our previous report [19], can cross the BBB and are accumulated mostly in neurons. Based on the qualitative analysis of the images, MW-40–7 samples showed more accumulation in neurons compared to MW-10–14 suggesting that smaller CDs may have been uptaken more by the neural cells; thus the size of the CDs influences the cell uptake. These results, combined with the zebrafish study support that MW-CDs have great potential for bioimaging for CNS-related injuries and diseases, especially ones involving neurons.

CDs are derived from d-glucose via two different synthetic routes, namely, microwave-assisted (MW) and hydrothermal carbonization (HT). Five different conditions were used for each of the synthetic routes changing the duration or temperature of the reaction, and the concentration of the starting material. Having one precursor was advantageous while comparing the effects of each parameter on the CD structure. Based on extensive characterization, we propose two different formation mechanisms for HT and MW samples which, as a result have different structures, thereby classifying them as two different subtypes of CDs.

UV/vis bands were the same regions for all samples. PL emission of the MW samples and HT samples were all excitation dependent and similar at low concentrations. The lower and higher limits of the reaction conditions, such as MW-10–5 and HT-120–5 samples resulted in different PL maxima as compared to the other samples, suggesting that at these conditions the percentage of CDs formed might be lower. In other words, CDs might start to form so that there might be other smaller fluorophores in the mixture that tend to emit in the blue range. Therefore, the emission maximum had a blue shift and higher intensity as compared to the other samples. This model is supported by TEM and AFM data as no particles could be detected for the MW-10–5 sample. Furthermore, MW-CDs samples showed a PL emission enhancement with increasing concentration, whereas HT samples were quenched with the increasing concentration. There was no significant difference between the mean diameter sizes of the HT-150–5, HT-200–5 and HT-200–10 samples according to the TEM images and size distribution histograms. The HT-200–20 sample resulted in an increased diameter size that may have resulted from aggregation. Overall, the size of HT-CDs did not depend on the reaction conditions, as the reactions resulted in CDs of similar size. On the other hand, the size of MW-CDs seemed to depend on the reaction conditions. Changing the reaction time or precursor concentration resulted in CDs of significantly different sizes. AFM images of the samples with increased mean diameter didn’t show a drastic change in the z-direction, suggesting more of a disk-like structure rather than the conventional spherical CDs for the MW samples. On the other hand, extracted height profiles of the AFM images of HT samples were in good agreement with TEM results suggesting that HT samples form spherical particles with narrower size distribution. XRD patterns for both MW and HT samples were consistent with amorphous core structures. FTIR, XPS, TGA and Raman analysis showed structural differences between MW and HT samples. According to these findings, we propose different formation mechanisms for the HT and MW CDs. Differences in the structure result in functional differences as well. HT-200–5 and HT-160–5 samples showed specific anticancer effects towards MYCN amplified neuroblastoma cells while MW samples were highly biocompatible for both healthy and cancer cells. Moreover, MW CDs showed great potential for bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo.

Synthetic parameters such as reaction time and temperature were correlated with the degree of carbonization affecting the structural properties of CDs. On the other hand, the degree of carbonization reduced the photoluminescence intensities. An important difference between HT and MW synthetic methods is that PL emission spectra of MW-CDs are concentration-dependent. In accordance with our earlier report [19], CDs crossed the BBB and localized to neurons in the central nervous system. Here, we confirm and extend our findings that cells of neuronal origin, such as neuroblastoma, are also targeted by HT-GluCDs. Strikingly, one type of HT-GluCDs showed specific anticancer activity against a chemotherapy-resistant neuroblastoma cell line while being non-toxic towards healthy cell lines, indicating a great new potential for therapeutic applications. Future works should focus on animal studies of neuroblastoma using HT GluCDs with and without drug conjugations.
